summary: Gruber reviews Good Sudoku by Zach Gage, framing it through the lens of Gage as a software auteur. He argues that Gage belongs to a rare class of game designers whose work has a distinctive voice and aesthetic across all their titles. The premise of Good Sudoku is deceptively simple — it's just Sudoku, but executed with exceptional design and craftsmanship. Gruber laments that software doesn't have enough auteurs like Gage, and argues that great execution alone can be a sufficient novelty.
key_points: Zach Gage is a software auteur whose games share a distinctive voice and aesthetic regardless of premise, comparable to recognizing a Stephen King novel or Scorsese film by feel
Gage's games succeed through a combination of joy, craftsmanship, and originality — they're not just good games but good apps with excellent software design
Good Sudoku's novelty is purely in execution: taking a game with thousands of mediocre implementations and finally doing it with great design
Software lacks enough auteurs and Gruber doesn't know why, but considers it notable that Gage fills that role
money_quotes: There's a distinct Gage-yness to a Zach Gage game, in the way that you know, say, a Stephen King novel or Martin Scorsese movie just by the feel of it.
It's a combination of joy, craftsmanship, and originality.
I say craftsmanship because they're not just good games but they're good *apps*.
We don't have enough auteurs like that in software. I don't know why that is.
Good Sudoku is Sudoku with great design, consideration, and craftsmanship. That's it, and it turns out that's idea enough.
There are hundreds, maybe thousands of Sudoku video games, and none of them are great (and most of them stink).
key_insight: Great execution and craftsmanship alone can be sufficient novelty — software needs more auteurs who bring a distinctive voice and taste to everything they make.
